g.text: 15 APRIL 1912 · NORTH ATLANTIC · 41°43′57″N 49°56′49″W · SANK IN 2 HR 40 MIN
Bow rivet — wrought iron, HIGH SLAG
No single large gash. Multiple rivet-head failures
opened seams across 6 hull sections totalling
approximately 1.1 m² (12 sq ft) of total opening.
Steel rivets midship would likely have held.
Bulkheads reached E-deck (2nd from bottom) at best.
No transverse watertight deck connected their tops.
overruled on cost grounds by White Star Line.
Starboard bow scraped ~90 m at 22.5 kn. Rivets sheared across 6 sections. ~400 t/min ingress. No large gash.
Bow rivets: wrought iron, 9.3% MnS slag (max 2–3%). Sheared along brittle planes. Seams opened ~1.1 m² total.
Bulkheads reached E-deck only. No watertight deck cap. Designed for 4 flooded compartments; 5th was fatal.
Water overtopped bulkhead E into BR4 with no direct breach. Cascade made outcome unrecoverable by ~00:30 AM.
Hogging exceeded hull limit. Keel buckled (compression); upper decks failed (tension). Fractured between funnels 3–4.
S=0.069%, Mn:S=6.8:1, temp −2°C (DBTT). Charpy: 4–10 J vs 200+ J modern. Brittle fracture propagated under hogging.
Sources: NIST (1996) · SNAME Marine Technology Vol. 35 (1998) · Foecke & McCarty (2008) · MAIB formal investigation
